focus: Stress-test graph vocabulary and convergence gates: candidate saturation, BUG_CLASS/INVARIANT/PRODUCER/CONSUMER/TEST nodes, graphless fallback, and STOP_BLOCKED behavior.
summary: Graph/convergence expansion is useful but must be ledger-led: current review graph schema and workflow discard BUG_CLASS/INVARIANT/PRODUCER/CONSUMER/TEST kinds, current graph signals cannot measure candidate saturation, graphless STOP behavior contradicts the docs, and blocked_stop needs named candidate/search gates before graph blockers can prove depth.

graphEvents.label: Review graph vocabulary expansion is inert unless schema and workflow allow new candidate node kinds
Current review graph signals do not measure candidate saturation
Graphless STOP behavior contradicts graceful degradation docs

label: Review graph vocabulary expansion is inert unless schema and workflow allow new candidate node kinds
Current review graph signals do not measure candidate saturation
Graphless STOP behavior contradicts graceful degradation docs
Empty/no-finding graph states can continue without actionable candidate blockers
Seeded tests need graph vocabulary, graphless fallback, and candidate blocked-stop fixtures
The review upsert path keeps only DIMENSION, FILE, FINDING, EVIDENCE, and REMEDIATION events, so BUG_CLASS-style graph events need an allow-list and schema update before prompt rollout.
Candidate saturation must be validated from ledger/searchCoverage before graph projection can count as proof.
Graphless runs must either produce accepted fallback proof or a typed candidate-search blocker; empty graph CONTINUE alone is not enough.

direction: Add graph vocabulary before ledger semantics stabilize
reason: New graph node kinds would currently be discarded or lack enforceable row semantics; ledger validation must come first.

direction: Use existing review graph signals as candidate-saturation proof
reason: Existing signals measure dimensions, findings, P0 resolution, evidence density, and hotspots, not bug-class/invariant/producer-consumer/negative-test search coverage.
